speaker: interviewer
text: --95. And before we end the tape, I would like to ask you one question. And that is, what advice do you have for your daughter, your grandchildren, and for future generations, after having survived the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't presume to be that wise man to give advice. And as far as my daughter is concerned, she got my message very well, because she is continuing in keeping the memory of the Holocaust alive. She is really involved in our-- in the Cracow Society's organization, as a second generation survivor, in our programs to commemorate the Holocaust. But advice-- it's-- it's only common sense. If you disregard human-- a human life, other human beings-- if you don't-- if you cannot muster respect for others as you would like others to have for you, then you're already lost. What-- things that are going on now and in the world with ethnic cleansing in Bosnia, or any such things where there are atrocities perpetrated against other people, it's because of the disregard of-- of humanity. And I cannot see a person who would like to be treated in a certain way that they couldn't muster the same emotions with regard to other people. So my advice is be a-- be a mensch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Is there anything else you'd like to say before we end?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I covered mostly everything. I didn't go into a lot of details. But I'll tell you something. It's interesting that some of the details are fading away. I don't have that many details anymore. And details play a large part in this kind of a-- of a conversation and testimony. But it is 50 years. And I hear some people are endowed with better memories than I am. I know my brother that lives in Brooklyn, he's got a much better memory. He could tell you details, things that I would never be able to remember. But I don't have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well to conclude this, it-- it will really-- wouldn't be complete if I wouldn't be able to express my gratitude and my thanks to people like you, to organizations like the Spielberg Holocaust Visual Foundation, that it taking this-- that much interest, and really to preserve the memory of the Holocaust, which is so, so necessary to be able to refute all those deniers. Because there are so many of those deniers. And who knows? In 10 years from now, 50 years from now, they will-- they may have the upper hand, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these testimonies, once captured on film, these are-- they are the real testimonies that will-- will prevail, I hope. And people will know it. And people will learn from it. The most important thing-- learn from it. Thank you very much. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my dear mother. Her name is Rose Pesel Fruma Forestein Weingarten. She-- this picture was taken in 1918, in Cracow, Poland. She perished, together with my father and my two sisters, during the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] The picture on the left is my father, Baruch Josef Weingarten. It was taken at the beginning of the war, probably 1940. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he also, he perished together with my mother and my two sisters, during the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The picture next to it is a picture of the three brothers. I, myself, am to the left; the center is the youngest brother, Jacov, who lives in Israel; and next to him is Jechiel, who lives in Brooklyn. This was taken when I was approximately eight years old, and then the middle brother was about four, and then-- and two-- and four-- and-- and six, sorry. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] The document on the left is a document issued by UNRRA, in 1945, in Munich, soon after liberation, certifying that I was in Flossenburg concentration camp. And there is the number that they assigned to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you wait-- wait for me to film it. Should he say the number? [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS] OK. I just have to do one thing here. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] OK, and the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next document is a pass issued by American authorities in Munich, when I was working for the Jewish Joint Distribution Committee. And it entitled me-- I had access to the P-- to the-- what is this name of that? I don't remember. The-- where the military was able to buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: PX.

speaker: interviewee
text: PX. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Munich, Germany, approximately 1946, at the wedding of Erna Rosner to William Rosner. At the very left, there's a picture of my first wife. And second to her is myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say her name. Say her-- say her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Felice Weingarten. And next, is myself, Roman Weingarten. And the tall guy standing out there is Oskar Schindler. This picture was taken on the occasion of my grandson, Jonathan's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town--

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of-- of-- this was in January, 1972. The top row is-- the first lady is my cousin, Sasha. The next one is my sister, Lucia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lucia what?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- her name is Wald. Her-- her-- her married name is Wald-- Lucia Weingarten Wald.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my niece, married to-- to my nephew. What's his name? The-- this is the other niece, married to the-- my other nephew, Avram [NON-ENGLISH]. And this is my wife, Helena.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, in the other row, this is my brother, Jechiel. Next is his son, [NON-ENGLISH], and his older son, Avram [NON-ENGLISH] And this is myself, Roman Weingarten.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken on the occasion of my-- my granddaughter, Mary Ruth, bat mitzvah. Sitting on the left is myself, Roman Weingarten. Next to myself is my wife, Helena.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The little guy to my-- to my left, is Jonathan Slutzman. This, next to her-- or him, is my daughter, Bernice, the mother of Jonathan. Next to her is Mary Ruth, the bat mitzvah girl. And next to her is her father, my son-in-law, Stuart Slutzman. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] This book was published by the New Cracow Friendship Society in 1990, while I was still the president. And it is the work of myself and Norbert Friedman, who were the editors, and who put the whole thing together, on the occasion of the 25th anniversary of the establishment of the New Cracow Friendship Society. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This plaque was awarded to me in 1977. And I was honored for Israel bonds by the New Cracow Friendship Society. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This letter was sent to me in 1987, by then President Ronald Reagan, on the occasion of our celebration of the 25th anniversary of the organization that I was president, the New Cracow Friendship Society. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This hanukiah was given to me by the-- by the New Cracow Friendship Society, in appreciation and for my service as president between 1983 and 1989. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] This Torah scroll was presented to me by the Jewish Community Center of West Hempstead, on the occasion of my being honored for UJA Federation. [PAUSES FOR 78 SECONDS]
